label: Control of Fire
description: Homo sapiens' mastery of fire ~400,000 years ago, enabling cooked food (driving brain growth), warmth, light, and social bonding around hearths — arguably the first and most consequential technology in human history.

label: Writing Systems
description: Development of cuneiform (Sumeria ~3200 BCE), hieroglyphics (Egypt ~3100 BCE), and later alphabets — enabling record-keeping, law, debt, long-distance trade, and the transmission of knowledge across generations and geography.

label: Printing Press
description: Gutenberg's movable-type press (1440) that democratized text production, collapsing the cost of books by 98%, enabling the Protestant Reformation, scientific revolution, and Enlightenment by making ideas reproducible and contestable at scale.

label: Gunpowder & Firearms
description: Chinese invention of gunpowder (9th century) and its weaponization into firearms in Europe (14th century) that obsoleted feudal armor and castles, enabling centralized nation-states, colonial conquest, and the end of knight-based feudalism.

label: Scientific Revolution
description: 16th–17th century shift from authority-based to observation-based knowledge — Copernicus, Galileo, Kepler, Newton — establishing the heliocentric model, laws of motion, and experimental method as foundations of modern science.

label: Steam Engine
description: Watt's improved steam engine (1769) powering factories, railways, and ships — the mechanical heart of the Industrial Revolution that transformed energy, labor, urbanization, and colonialism on a planetary scale.

label: Industrial Revolution
description: 18th–19th century shift from agrarian to factory production, driven by steam power, mechanization, and capital accumulation — creating the modern working class, urban slums, global supply chains, and capitalism as we know it.

label: Telegraph
description: Samuel Morse's electric telegraph (1837) enabling near-instantaneous long-distance communication for the first time, collapsing the lag between events and knowledge of them — reshaping journalism, financial markets, military coordination, and empire.

label: Germ Theory of Disease
description: Pasteur and Koch's demonstration in the 1850s–70s that specific microorganisms cause specific diseases, enabling vaccines, antibiotics, surgery, and public health systems — the conceptual foundation of modern medicine.

label: Electrical Grid
description: Edison's Pearl Street Station (1882) and Tesla/Westinghouse's AC systems establishing distributed electrical power — transforming factories, homes, cities, and eventually all of modern infrastructure, enabling the 20th century's consumer economy.

label: Internal Combustion Engine
description: Otto's internal combustion engine (1876) and its application to automobiles (Benz 1885) and aircraft (Wright 1903), restructuring cities around cars, enabling oil-dependent global logistics, and making petroleum the strategic resource of the 20th century.

label: Radio & Wireless Communications
description: Marconi's wireless telegraphy (1895) and broadcast radio enabling mass simultaneous communication for the first time — weaponized by both democracies and fascists for propaganda, used by FDR's fireside chats and Nazi Volksempfänger alike.

label: Nuclear Weapons
description: Manhattan Project producing atomic bombs dropped on Hiroshima and Nagasaki (1945), killing 200,000+ and initiating the nuclear age — reshaping geopolitics through mutual assured destruction, arms races, and the permanent specter of civilizational destruction.

label: Transistor & Semiconductor Revolution
description: Bell Labs transistor invention (1947) enabling miniaturization of electronics, leading to integrated circuits, microprocessors, personal computers, smartphones, and the digital economy — the foundational component of the information age.

label: Green Revolution
description: Norman Borlaug's development of high-yield dwarf wheat varieties and agrochemical-intensive farming in the 1960s–70s that dramatically increased food production, preventing famine for hundreds of millions while creating pesticide dependency, groundwater depletion, and smallholder displacement.

label: Internet
description: ARPANET (1969) → World Wide Web (1991) → mass adoption — a decentralized packet-switching network that became the substrate for global communication, commerce, disinformation, social movements, and surveillance capitalism.

label: Personal Computer
description: Apple II, IBM PC, and Macintosh placing computing power in homes and offices, democratizing information processing, enabling the software industry, and beginning the shift from industrial to knowledge economy.

label: Mobile Phone & Smartphone
description: From Motorola DynaTAC (1983) to iPhone (2007), mobile phones becoming pocket-sized internet terminals carried by 6+ billion people — enabling social movements, surveillance, instant payments, misinformation, and always-on connectivity.

label: Social Media Platforms
description: Facebook (2004), Twitter (2006), YouTube (2005), Instagram (2010) — attention-economy platforms optimizing for engagement through algorithmic amplification of outrage, enabling both democratic organizing and radicalization pipelines at scale.

label: GPS & Satellite Systems
description: US military GPS constellation (1978, civilian 1983) enabling precision navigation for missiles, autonomous vehicles, logistics, smartphones, and precision agriculture — also the foundation of drone warfare and mass surveillance.

label: CRISPR Gene Editing
description: Jennifer Doudna and Emmanuelle Charpentier's CRISPR-Cas9 system (2012) enabling precise, cheap gene editing — opening paths to curing genetic diseases, engineering crops, and creating heritable human genetic modifications, with profound ethical stakes.

label: Machine Learning & AI
description: Deep learning revolution from AlexNet (2012) through GPT (2020+), enabling image recognition, language generation, and autonomous systems at superhuman performance — reshaping labor, warfare, surveillance, creativity, and the nature of knowledge.

label: Mass Surveillance Technology
description: Post-9/11 explosion of government and corporate surveillance: NSA bulk data collection, facial recognition systems, social credit systems, predictive policing, and location tracking — enabled by cheap sensors, big data, and algorithmic processing.

label: Renewable Energy Revolution
description: Solar and wind power costs falling 90%+ from 2010–2024, making clean energy the cheapest source of new electricity generation in most of the world — the fastest energy transition in history, racing against climate tipping points.

label: Vaccines
description: Jenner's smallpox vaccine (1796) through modern mRNA vaccines (COVID-19, 2020) — arguably the highest return-on-investment public health technology in history, eliminating smallpox, nearly eliminating polio, and saving hundreds of millions of lives.

label: Antibiotics
description: Fleming's discovery of penicillin (1928) and mass production in WWII enabling treatment of bacterial infections that had killed half of all humans who ever lived from sepsis and pneumonia — while overuse created antibiotic-resistant superbugs threatening the entire framework.

label: Nuclear Power
description: First nuclear power plant (USSR, 1954) offering carbon-free baseload electricity, but linked to weapons proliferation fears, waste storage problems, and catastrophic accident risk (Chernobyl 1986, Fukushima 2011) — a technology whose social acceptance has never recovered.

label: Fossil Fuel Economy
description: Coal → oil → natural gas economy powering industrial civilization from 1850 to present — enabling unprecedented prosperity and also the accumulation of 420 ppm CO2, committing Earth to centuries of warming and the climate crisis.

label: Drone Warfare
description: Remotely piloted and autonomous weapons systems enabling targeted killing without pilot risk — lowering the political cost of military action, enabling signature strikes against unknown individuals, and normalizing autonomous lethal decision-making.

label: Blockchain & Cryptocurrency
description: Bitcoin (2009) and subsequent cryptocurrencies creating decentralized ledgers and programmable money — enabling both permissionless financial access and darknet markets, ransomware, energy-intensive speculation, and challenges to monetary sovereignty.
